text:
Understanding the structure of a catalyst under working conditions is crucial for elucidating the factors that determine its performance. The structure of a catalyst is not invariant in going from the synthesis state to the working catalytic state and therefore in situ studies are generally the most meaningful. Here, in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) is employed to elucidate the structure of $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ hollow nanoframes while in operation as a world-class catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR).

To investigate the surface properties of the nanoframes, the characteristics of the electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition (Hupd) and carbon monoxide (CO) electrooxidation are compared, and the influence of the structure of nanoframes on the adsorbate binding strength is studied. The influence of the Pt-skin on the oxidation reaction rate (ORR) and the surface binding energy is investigated. Through the comparative analysis of the ex situ and in situ XAS of the nanoframes, the surface characteristics of the nanoframes with strong and weak adsorbate binding are obtained, and the relationship between the ORR activity and the surface characteristics is revealed. It is found that the nanoframes with strong adsorbate binding have a rougher Pt surface, and the insufficient Pt segregation and Ni dissolution lead to a lower ORR activity. Conversely, the nanoframes with the highest ORR activity show the highest degree of Pt segregation to the Ni-rich subsurface layers, indicating the formation of a high-quality Pt-skin. The superior performance of the $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ nanoframes is determined to depend on the precise control of the surface structure of the Pt-skin.
explanation: The text is very cohesive and uses polished academic phrasing, with some promotional wording ('world-class catalyst'), which can indicate AI authorship. The technical content is plausible for a real study.
